signals: Treatment with one of four approved anti-influenza drugs may be considered if the patient presents within 48 hours
benefit of treatment is greatest when antiviral therapy is started within 24 hours
These drugs decrease the duration of illness by about 24 hours
may decrease the risk of serious complications

extraction.summary: Influenza is an acute viral respiratory infection causing significant morbidity and mortality worldwide. The hallmark of infection is abrupt onset of fever, cough, chills or sweats, myalgias, and malaise. Treatment with antiviral drugs within 48 hours, ideally within 24 hours, can reduce symptom duration by approximately 24 hours.

population.age_criteria: All ages, with special attention to children younger than 5 years (particularly those younger than 2 years) and adults 65 years and older
population.risk_criteria: High-risk groups include those with chronic conditions (asthma, heart disease, diabetes, immunosuppression), pregnant and postpartum women, institutionalized adults, American Indians and Alaska Natives, and those with neuromuscular diseases
population.disease_definition: Acute viral respiratory infection with abrupt onset of fever, cough, chills or sweats, myalgias, and malaise
population.exclusions: None specified for diagnosis; contraindications exist for specific treatments based on allergies and comorbidities
population.severity_staging: Uncomplicated influenza vs. severe disease with respiratory failure, secondary bacterial pneumonia, or extrapulmonary complications

key_thresholds.context: Maximum time from symptom onset for antiviral effectiveness
Time from symptom onset for maximum clinical benefit
High risk threshold for empiric treatment consideration
Typical duration of viral shedding after symptom onset
Single dose for children 2-12 years (up to 600 mg maximum)
Single dose for adults and children ≥13 years
Laboratory-confirmed cases within 72 hours in long-term care facility

recommendations.recommendation: Annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all people 6 months and older
The decision to begin antiviral treatment should be based on clinical diagnosis of influenza, not on test results
recommendations.notes: Laboratory diagnosis obtained when results will change management

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with severe illness or high risk of complications should receive antiviral treatment regardless of symptom duration
Oseltamivir, peramivir, baloxavir, or zanamivir may be considered to reduce symptom duration if administered within 48 hours of symptom onset
recommendations.notes: Ideally within 24 hours for maximum benefit

recommendations.recommendation: Oral oseltamivir is the preferred treatment for influenza during pregnancy
Initiate antiviral treatment within 48 hours of symptom onset for optimal efficacy
recommendations.notes: Treatment reduces symptom duration by approximately one day

recommendations.recommendation: Use oseltamivir as first-line treatment for pregnant women
recommendations.notes: Good safety data in pregnancy, no dosing adjustment needed

recommendations.recommendation: Do not use multiple antiviral agents or higher than recommended dosages
recommendations.notes: No demonstrated benefit to combination therapy

recommendations.recommendation: Consider extended treatment courses in critically ill patients
recommendations.notes: Based on clinical judgment for severe cases

recommendations.recommendation: Do not routinely use corticosteroids unless other approved indication exists
Consider chemoprophylaxis for specific high-risk populations and outbreak settings
recommendations.notes: Most indications based on poor-quality evidence and expert opinion

medications.dose: Adults: 75mg twice daily for 5 days; Children: weight-based dosing
medications.indication: Treatment and prevention of influenza A and B
Treatment and prevention of influenza A and B

medications.dose: Adults: 600mg single dose IV; Children: 12mg/kg single dose IV
medications.indication: Treatment of uncomplicated influenza A and B
Treatment of uncomplicated influenza in patients ≥12 years

red_flags: Myocarditis or ischemic heart disease complications
Development of lower respiratory tract disease while on antivirals (consider treatment resistance)
extraction.follow_up: Monitor for complications in high-risk patients; consider return visit if symptoms worsen or persist beyond expected duration; watch for secondary bacterial infections
extraction.escalation_path: Hospitalization indicated for severe illness, respiratory failure, or high-risk patients with complications; consider ICU for multiorgan failure or severe pneumonia; infectious disease consultation for complicated cases or treatment failures
